paper.title: Characterisation of high velocity stars in the S-PLUS internal fourth data release

extraction.summary: The paper analyzes 64 high-velocity stars selected with V_GC > 400 km/s from Gaia DR3 and S-PLUS iDR4. The abstract and conclusions explicitly state all 64 stars are gravitationally bound to the Galaxy. Only Gaia DR3 2690227738799604224 appears marginally above escape velocity curves, but the authors conclude its radial velocity is likely spurious (SNR=3.2 < 5). In the Appendix, Gaia DR3 3631125674577631104 is noted as an unbound candidate under StarHorse distances but with large uncertainty. No star is identified as an HVS or unbound candidate by the paper.

method_chain.summary: Selection of stars with Galactocentric velocity V_GC > 400 km/s from Gaia DR3 full phase-space sample, cross-matched with S-PLUS iDR4 to yield 64 stars.

source_refs.context: Section 3: Selection of stars with V_GC > 400 km/s, cross-match with S-PLUS iDR4

method_chain.summary: Astrometric quality cuts: parallax > 0, RUWE < 1.4, -3 < astrometric_gof_al < 3, fidelity_v2 > 0.5, norm_dg < -3 (removes colour contamination).

source_refs.context: Section 3: Quality cuts on Gaia DR3 astrometric parameters

method_chain.summary: Galactocentric velocity V_GC computed using Astropy v4.0 Galactocentric frame: Sun-GC distance 8.122 kpc (Gravity Collaboration 2018), Sun height 20.8 pc (Bennett & Bovy 2019), solar velocity (12.9, 245.6, 7.78) km/s (Drimmel & Poggio 2018). Distances from Bailer-Jones et al. (2021) photo-geometric distances. MC uncertainty propagation (1000 realizations) using Pyia.

source_refs.context: Section 3: Galactocentric frame transformation and MC uncertainty propagation

method_chain.summary: Comparison of V_GC vs R_GC to escape velocity curves from three Galactic potentials: Irrgang et al. (2013) model I, McMillan (2017), and MWPotential2014 (Bovy 2015) with heavy-halo variant (1.2e12 Msun). All 64 stars found below escape velocity curves except Gaia DR3 2690227738799604224 (attributed to spurious radial velocity, SNR=3.2 < 5).

source_refs.context: Section 3: Escape velocity comparison; all stars bound except Gaia DR3 2690227738799604224 (spurious RV)

method_chain.summary: Backwards orbit integration over 10 Gyr using galpy with McMillan (2017) potential. Additional barred potential investigation using AGAMA (Sormani et al. 2022 barred potential, pattern speed 35.5 km/s/kpc, bar inclination 30 deg). Orbital energies confirm all stars are bound.
outputs: Orbital parameters: eccentricity, apocenter, pericenter, energy

source_refs.context: Section 4.1: Orbit integration with galpy and AGAMA

method_chain.summary: Chemical tagging using [Fe/H], [alpha/Fe], [Mg/Fe] from GALAH DR3 (11 stars) and LAMOST DR8 LR. CNN-derived [Mg/Fe] for metal-rich stars. Identified Gaia DR3 5401875170994688896 as possible GC origin candidate; some stars as possible Sequoia and GES remnants.

source_refs.context: Section 5.2 and Conclusions: Chemical analysis and origin assessment

candidate_groups_considered.description: 64 high-velocity stars selected with V_GC > 400 km/s from Gaia DR3 with full phase-space information, cross-matched with S-PLUS iDR4. Listed in Table 2 (astrometric parameters) with further data in Tables 3-5.
candidate_groups_considered.reason: All 64 stars are gravitationally bound to the Galaxy as explicitly stated in the abstract (line 64: 'All the stars are gravitationally bound to the Galaxy') and conclusions (line 557: 'all but star Gaia DR3 2690227738799604224 are bound to the MW'). Gaia DR3 2690227738799604224 appears marginally above escape velocity curves but its radial velocity is likely spurious (SNR=3.2 < 5, parallax_over_error ~ 6). Gaia DR3 3631125674577631104 appears as unbound candidate under StarHorse distances but with large uncertainty (Appendix). No star qualifies as an HVS or unbound candidate.

source_refs.context: Abstract: 'All the stars are gravitationally bound to the Galaxy and exhibit halo kinematics.'
Section 3: 'Except for the star Gaia DR3 2690227738799604224...all other stars are bound to the Galaxy...there is a high chance of its radial velocity being spurious.'
Conclusions: 'all but star Gaia DR3 2690227738799604224 are bound to the MW...the star probably has an unreliable galactocentric velocity.'
Appendix: 'the star Gaia DR3 3631125674577631104 is an unbound candidate if we consider the StarHorse distance, but with a large uncertainty in V_GC.'
